qualification_assessment.qualification_basis: The core Hebrew terms tsa.rah (H6869B) and tsar (H6862B) directly name the inner experience of being pressed and narrowed from within, as evidenced in Psa 31:7 ('you have known the distress of my soul') and Jon 2:2 ('out of my distress I called'); the Greek thlipsis and adēmoneō confirm the inner dimension across the NT.

term_inventory.meaning_summary: Primarily a being held together, compression; metaphorically distress of mind, anxiety.
Pressure, distress; derived from root tsuq (to press).
Bad, evil, harmful; covers physical harm, moral evil, and emotional distress (sad, unhappy, displeasing).
To pain bodily or mentally; passive: to be in agony, be tormented, be distressed.
Passive: to be overwhelmed with wonder, distressed, alarmed; to be amazed, astonished, awe-struck.
Aramaic equivalent of ba.ash (to stink); P'al: to be evil, bad, displeasing.
To be troubled, distressed, depressed or dejected, full of anguish or sorrow.
Ithp'el: to be distressed, be grieved.
To bind, be distressed, be in distress, be cramped, be narrow, be scant, be in straits; Qal: to suffer distress.
Qal: to be displeasing, to be sad, to be injurious or evil; Hiphil: to do injury or harm, to act wickedly.
Bitter, bitterness; of water or food; figuratively of harlot's end, pain, cry; as adverb: bitterly.
Constraint, distress; from the root tsuq (to press).
Dearth, destitution; appears in contexts of times of trouble (Psa 9:9; 10:1).
Ruin, disaster, destruction; used in wisdom contexts of calamity befalling the wicked.
Straitness, straits, distress, stress, anguish; from root tsuq (to press).
Compression, distress, pressure; from root uq/aqah (to totter, pressure).
Straits, distress, trouble; the primary OT noun for inner distress in crisis; used especially in lament and prayer contexts.
Straits, distress; abstract noun from the spatial root meaning narrowness and constriction.
Constraint, distress, strait; figuratively anguish; from root tsuq.
Evil, misery, distress, injury; moral evil and calamity brought on by wickedness.
Evil, misery, distress, injury; harm and calamity as external threat or divine judgment.
Adversary, foe, enemy, oppressor; same root as tsar (distress) — the one who creates the narrow place.
Trouble, wickedness, sorrow; covers trouble and sorrow as well as idolatry and iniquity.
Hard pebble, flint; concrete physical meaning only.
Narrowness of place; metaphorically straits, distress, anguish, hardship, difficulty.
Narrow, tight; the concrete spatial meaning that underlies the metaphorical distress terms.
Necessity, constraint, compulsion; also distress, trial, affliction, hardship; bodily pain and anguish.
Cry, outcry; cry of distress, outcry and clamour; the vocalised expression of inner distress.
Outcry, shout; the vocalised expression of distress or lamentation.
Pain (mental and physical), sorrow; covers both bodily and inner pain without distinction.
Primarily pressure, compression; metaphorically affliction, distress of mind, distressing circumstances, trial.
Strife, controversy, dispute; primarily legal dispute and quarrel; marginally related to inner distress.

term_inventory.anchor_grammatical_form: not available — wrong word analysis block in source file
term_inventory.meaning_summary: Not available from source file — word analysis block contains riv (H7379) data in error; three verses present (Psa 116:3; 118:5; Lam 1:3) indicate meaning: distress, anguish, narrow place.
Qal: to burn, kindle (anger); Niphal: to be angry, incensed; Hiphil: to burn, kindle; Hithpael: to heat oneself in vexation.
Qal: to confine, secure; to shut in, besiege; to shut up, enclose; primarily military siege language.
Qal: to bind, tie up, be scant, be cramped; Pual: to be bound; Hiphil: to make narrow, cause distress, press hard upon.
To disturb, alarm, terrify, hurry; Niphal: to be disturbed, dismayed, terrified, anxious, or to be in haste; Piel and Hiphil: to dismay, terrify, make haste.
To suffer distress; the experiential register of the tsa.rar root focused on inner suffering.
Active: to cause sorrow, grief, distress; passive: to be sorrowful, sad, distressed; covers both causing and experiencing grief.
Qal: to be hard, difficult, severe; Niphal: to be ill-treated, hard pressed; Piel: severe labour; Hiphil: to make difficult, make stubborn, harden (especially heart).
Primarily to hold together; to confine, constrain, compel; passive: to suffer, be distressed, be seized with; covers physical illness, mental constriction, and constraining influence.
Qal: to hurt, pain; Niphal: to be in pain, pained, grieved; Piel: to vex, torture; Hiphil: to cause pain; Hithpael: to feel grieved, be vexed.
Qal: to feel a loathing at, abhor; to feel a sickening dread; Hiphil: to cause sickening dread, cause loathing.
Qal: to complain, to muse/meditate/ponder, to talk/sing/speak; Polel: to meditate, consider, put forth thoughts.
Passive: to be oppressed, distressed; to exhaust by labour or suffering; to wear out, distress; to overpower, oppress.
Qal: to press, drive, oppress, exact, exert demanding pressure; Niphal: to be hard pressed.
Qal: to be in physical or mental pain; Hiphil: to cause pain, hurt, mar.
Hiphil: to constrain, press, bring into straits, straiten, oppress.
To squeeze, press; to press upon, encumber, throng; metaphorically to distress, afflict; passive: to be compressed, narrow.
To crowd together into a narrow place; passive: to be in straits, be cooped up, be cramped from action.
Qal: to show hostility to, be an adversary, treat as foe.
To make a rival-wife; the social/relational register of the tsa.rar root in a polygamous marriage context.
To bother, annoy; passive: to be harassed; metaphorically in sorry plight, worn out.

Qal: to dance, twist, writhe, whirl; Polel: to writhe in travail, bear, wait anxiously; Hithpolel: whirling, writhing in torture, wait longingly; Hithpalpel: to be distressed. Covers physical writhing and inner anguish.
To show hostility toward, vex; the interpersonal/adversarial register of the tsa.rar root.
Vexer, rival wife; the person who causes distress in a polygamous marriage context.

cross_registry_links.note: cha.rah is listed under Distress and its related word burning anger (cha.ron) is the noun form of the anger root; the term set of Distress and Anger share this root.
lupeō appears in the Distress term set and the related word grief (lupē) is the noun form that directly names grief; the two words share this Greek root family.
odunaō and its related noun odunē (anguish) bridge the Distress and Grief semantic fields in NT usage.
mar appears in the Distress STEP term set; bitterness of soul is a recurring expression linking Distress and Bitter across the Psalms and lament literature.
The ke.ev/ka.av root covering mental and physical pain appears in both Distress and would appear in a Pain word entry; Jer 15:18 and Job 2:13 show the overlap.
Both anguish terms in the Distress set connect directly to a likely separate Anguish entry in the 181.
ba.hal covers both terror/dismay and haste; its related word be.ha.lah (dismay) and its overlap with fear terms makes it a cross-registry bridge.
The ra.ah terms cover evil and misery that in several verses (Neh 1:3; Est 8:6) intersect with shame and reproach contexts already identified in the Shame analysis.
Manasseh's distress leading to humbling before God — the tsa.rah verse set contains examples of distress as the trigger for humility.
The hardening/stiffening of the neck in qa.shah verses connects directly to Pride as the inner disposition that resists correction and produces distress.

data_quality_flags.description: The word analysis block for me.tsar (H4712) contains the heading, meaning section, related words, and occurrence count for riv (H7379 strife) — this appears to be a copy-paste error in the source file; only the three verse references are correctly attributed to me.tsar.
lupēros (G3077) distressing appears in the STEP related word list at the top of the source file but has no STEP block in the body of the file; no verses or meaning data are available.

me.tsu.qah (metsuwqah) appears in the primary term list at the top of the source file with gloss 'distress, tightness, pressure' and reference Deut 28:53 / Isa 8:22, but has no dedicated STEP block; it appears only as a related word within other term entries.

ra.ah appears as H7451I (distress: evil) and H7451C (distress: harm) — both show identical occurrence counts (684x) and near-identical meaning sections; kept as separate entries per analysis instruction to preserve granularity for Phase 2.

tsa.rar appears as four sub-entries H6887B (to constrain), H6887C (to distress), H6887D (to vex), H6887E (to rival) — all from the same root; kept separate per instruction; contextual analysis reveals genuinely different registers across the four sub-entries.

tsar appears as four sub-entries H6862A (narrow), H6862B (distress), H6862C (enemy), H6862D (hard) — same root with distinct glosses; kept separate; sub-entries show genuinely different primary usages ranging from physical space to inner experience to adversarial relationship.
tsoq (H6695A) has 1 occurrence with 1 verse (Dan 9:25); the verse context is temporal/historical and the single occurrence is classified PERIPHERAL, limiting inner-being analysis.
mu.a.qah (H4157) has 1 occurrence with 1 verse (Psa 66:11); the verse is CORE but the single data point cannot establish patterns.
ke.ra (H3735) has 1 occurrence with 1 verse (Dan 7:15); the verse is CORE but single data point limits analysis.
tsa.rar as rival (H6887E) has 1 occurrence with 1 verse (Lev 18:18); classified PERIPHERAL — this sub-entry contributes no inner-being data.
tsa.rah as vexer (H6869C) has 1 occurrence with 1 verse (1Sa 1:6); classified CORE — the single verse is Hannah's story and is significantly rich despite the single occurrence.
kataponeō (G2669) has 2 occurrences with 2 verses; the passive form distressed by oppression is available in 2Pe 2:7 but the evidence base is thin.

stenochōreō (G4729) has 3 occurrences but only 1 verse (2Cor 6:12) appears in the STEP block; the inner-being dimension in this single verse focuses on restricted affections rather than external distress.
tsar as hard/flint (H6862D) has 1 occurrence (Isa 5:28) which is entirely physical; this sub-entry contributes no inner-being data and its presence in the term set is due to root family inclusion.

tsa.rar as rival wife (H6887E) has 1 occurrence (Lev 18:18) in a legal regulation; while the social context of rivalry produces distress (as seen in 1Sa 1:6 under tsa.rah H6869C), this specific sub-entry carries no inner-being content.

The tsa.rar root sub-entries (H6887B/C/D/E), when examined across their verse contexts, reveal four genuinely distinct registers: physical binding/siege (B), inner suffering experience (C), interpersonal hostility/vexing (D), and marital rivalry (E) — suggesting the STEP sub-division captures real semantic differentiation within the root rather than arbitrary lexical splitting.

The tsar root sub-entries (H6862A/B/C/D) similarly reveal a spatial-to-experiential spectrum: the concrete narrow/tight (A) underlies the abstract distress (B), which is embodied in the person of the enemy (C), and the physical hard/flint (D) represents a separate concrete usage with no experiential overlap — the root family traces a metaphorical journey from physical narrowness to inner experience.
